planting.note: Start transplants after 4 weeks cold-moist stratification (or direct sow in fall), in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Colorado blue columbine tolerates more sun in cool mountain climates but requires afternoon shade in zone 6–8 heat.

directions: Start transplants after 4 weeks cold-moist stratification (or direct sow in fall), in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Colorado blue columbine tolerates more sun in cool mountain climates but requires afternoon shade in zone 6–8 heat. Aquilegia caerulea is the Colorado state flower and one of the most beautiful native columbines, with large flowers (2–3 inches) of clear, sky-blue sepals and pure-white, flat petals with long, straight blue spurs — a bicolor combination of exceptional purity; it grows naturally in mountain meadows and open forests from Wyoming to New Mexico at elevations of 6,000–11,500 feet and blooms in June–August. It is shorter-lived than some columbines (typically 3–5 years) but self-seeds freely to maintain the colony; remove spent flowers only partially to allow sufficient seed to set. The long spurs are adapted to hawkmoth pollination, and it is an important nectar source for several sphinx moth species.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in consistently moist to saturated, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun at the margins of streams, ponds, or bog gardens; caltha leptosepala grows along snowmelt streams and is one of the earliest bloomers in mountain gardens, often flowering while snow still remains nearby.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in consistently moist to saturated, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun at the margins of streams, ponds, or bog gardens; caltha leptosepala grows along snowmelt streams and is one of the earliest bloomers in mountain gardens, often flowering while snow still remains nearby. Caltha leptosepala is the white-flowered western counterpart to the familiar yellow marsh marigold (C. palustris), native to cold, high-elevation bogs, stream margins, and subalpine meadows from Alaska to New Mexico; it produces large, kidney-shaped, glossy, dark-green leaves and glistening, white, buttercup-like flowers 1–1.5 inches across with a central cluster of yellow stamens in early spring, often among the very first wildflowers to bloom after snowmelt. It is less commonly grown than C. palustris but adapts well to water garden edges and bog gardens in zones 3–7 where it blooms several weeks earlier than most spring perennials.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed immediately after harvest in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds have complex dormancy requirements (double dormancy requiring one warm then one cold period) and germinate in the second spring after sowing — purchasing nursery transplants is faster.

directions: Direct sow fresh seed immediately after harvest in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds have complex dormancy requirements (double dormancy requiring one warm then one cold period) and germinate in the second spring after sowing — purchasing nursery transplants is faster. Clayton's sweet cicely is a native woodland herb of eastern North American deciduous forests that is nearly identical in appearance and use to Osmorhiza longistylis and O. berteroi; it produces large, feathery, compound leaves with a strong, sweet anise-licorice fragrance when crushed, and small, white, flat-topped flower clusters (umbels) in spring that are excellent for native bees and beneficial insects. The roots and leaves are edible with a mild anise flavor, historically used by Native Americans; it is an excellent, fragrant, deep-shade groundcover that naturalizes quietly under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; cuckoo flower requires consistent moisture and is ideal for rain gardens, pond margins, and wet, partially shaded spots in the garden.

directions: Direct sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; cuckoo flower requires consistent moisture and is ideal for rain gardens, pond margins, and wet, partially shaded spots in the garden. Cardamine pratensis is a delicate, early-spring wildflower of European wet meadows and stream banks, naturalized in northeastern North America; it produces rosettes of pinnate leaves and loose racemes of small, pale lilac-pink to white, four-petaled flowers from March through May, among the earliest spring blooms to appear in moist lowland meadows. It is the primary larval host plant for the Orange-tip butterfly (Anthocharis cardamines) in Europe and is an important early nectar source for queen bumblebees and small native bees; the double-flowered cultivar 'Flore Pleno' is popular in ornamental gardens. The leaves are edible with a mild cress-like flavor, similar to watercress.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild chervil self-seeds prolifically and can become invasive in disturbed sites — thin seedlings to prevent overcrowding.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild chervil self-seeds prolifically and can become invasive in disturbed sites — thin seedlings to prevent overcrowding. NOTE: Anthriscus sylvestris closely resembles Conium maculatum (poison hemlock) and Oenanthe crocata (hemlock water dropwort), both deadly poisonous; always positively identify by the red-flushed hollow stems and distinctive aroma before handling. Anthriscus sylvestris is a widespread European biennial or short-lived perennial that produces large, finely cut, fern-like, bright-green leaves from spring through early summer and frothy, flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers on tall stems that are spectacular bee and hoverfly forage in May and June; the ornamental dark-leafed cultivar 'Ravenswing' has deep, chocolate-burgundy foliage that is one of the most striking dark-leaved plants for partial shade in cool-climate gardens. The young leaves and stems have a mild anise-parsley flavor and are edible as a salad herb before flowering, though positive plant ID is essential.
